name: Homemade fish & chips
description: Proper old-school battered fish, chunky chips and comforting mushy peas - homemade heaven! A traditional British dish with crispy beer batter, fluffy chips and mint-flavoured mushy peas.
ingredients: 225g plain flour, plus extra for dusting
225g white fish fillets, skin off, pin-boned, from sustainable sources
A few sprigs of fresh mint
method: Peel and slice the potatoes into chips.
To make the mushy peas, pick and finely chop the mint leaves.
Place the butter in a pan over a medium-low heat, add the peas and mint, pop the lid on and simmer gently for 10 minutes.

Add a squeeze of lemon juice and season to taste with sea salt and black pepper - you can either mush the peas up in a food processor, or mash them by hand until stodgy, thick and perfect for dipping your fish into. Keep them warm until needed.
Heat the sunflower oil in a deep fryer or a large pan to 190°C/375°F.
Meanwhile, parboil the chips in boiling salted water for 4 to 5 minutes, or until softened but still retaining their shape, then drain and steam dry.
For the batter, mix the flour and baking powder in a bowl with a pinch of salt, then gradually whisk in the beer until you have a smooth batter.
Dust each fish fillet with some extra flour, then dip into the batter and let any excess drip off.
Carefully lower the fish into the oil one by one - don't overcrowd the fryer or pan.
Cook for 4 minutes, or until the fish is cooked through and the batter is golden and crisp, then remove to kitchen paper to drain.
When the chips are nice and dry, fry in the oil that the fish were cooked in at 180°C/350°F until golden and crisp.

While the chips are frying, transfer the fish from the kitchen paper to a baking tray. Place in the oven for a few minutes to finish cooking - this way they will stay crisp while you finish off the chips.
When the chips are done, drain them on kitchen paper, season with salt, and serve with the fish and mushy peas.
Serve with crunchy sweet pickled gherkins, pickled onions, pickled chillies, cheap malt vinegar and Heinz tomato ketchup.
